rulings.Case Title: LEONARD I. SHAVIES VS. FCA US LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion & Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF LEONARD SHAVIES' Motion For Attorneys Fees. Plaintiff Shavies' motion for recovery of attorney's fees is GRANTED. Shavies is entitled to reasonable attorney's fees as the prevailing part in this Song-Beverly Act action. (CC 1794(d).) The court determines that the Knight Law Group's use of numerous lawyers to litigate this lemon law case was excessive and reduce its fees by 20% (Total recovery = $10,560). The court determines that Mr. Rosenstein's claimed fees are reasonable except that he should not recover $1,650 for traveling to San Francisco for trial call after this case settled. Rosenstein should recover $38,962.60. The court further finds that based on the contingent nature of this case, its difficulty and plaintiff's success, Shavies should recover a multiplier of .2 Shavies therefore is entitled to a total of $59,427.12. This motion does not address Shavies' request for costs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Shavies is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE ROBERTS VS. SWANROCK INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTS COLIN SWAN, JOEL FRANGQUIST, SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Swanrock, Inc. dba Skytech Solar, Colin Swan, and Joel Frangquist's demurrer to the intentional misrepresentation cause of action is SUSTAINED with 20 days leave to amend. Roberts has leave to allege specific facts as to each defendant to support the intentional misrepresentation claim and/or plead a concealment claim per CACI 1901. It appears that the gravamen of the claim is that defendants actively concealed Learned's unlicensed status to solicit Roberts and that caused her damage. The demurrer to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. "Whether a fiduciary duty exists is generally a question of law." (Marzec v. Public Employees' Retirement System (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 889, 915.) Here, the contractor-hirer relationship is not a recognized fiduciary relationship such as guardian-ward, trustee-beneficiary, etc. (See Richelle L. v. Roman Catholic Archbishop (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 257, 271.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE ROBERTS VS. SWANROCK INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTS COLIN SWAN, JOEL FRANGQUIST, SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR'S MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Swanrock, Inc. dba Skytech Solar, Colin Swan, and Joel Frangquist's motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is GRANTED with 20 days leave to amend. Roberts has leave to plead a claim such as concealment that can support the prayer for punitive damages. Roberts' allegations that defendants refused to repair the roof and/or abandoned her are not sufficient to support the claim as a matter of law per McDonnell v. American Trust Co. (1955) 130 Cal.App.2d 296. rulings.Case Title: AARON DRENBERG VS. JAMES K. SONG JR.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT JAMES SONG, JR.'S MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT. Defendant James Song, Jr.'s motion to set default and vacate default judgment is GRANTED. Plaintiff Aaron Drenberg failed to show that the address on which substitute service was purportedly effected was the "dwelling house or usual place of abode" of Song. Although, Drenberg has submitted evidence showing that Song stayed at his parents' house, when in the US, that evidence does not refute Song's declaration that he did not reside at the address at the relevant time. (Song Decl. 19-23). The proof of service filed does not purport to show that substitute service was made at the usual mailing address of Song. The default and default judgment are vacated pursuant to CCP § 473(d). (See Hearn v. Howard (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1193, 1200, "[b]ecause the law favors disposing of cases on their merits, 'any doubts in applying section 473' must be resolved in favor of the party seeking relief from default.") Song shall file and serve a responsive pleading within 20 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Song is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. MICHAEL LIPSON ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT MICHAEL LIPSON'S MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT. Defendant Michael Lipson's motion to vacate default judgment is GRANTED and his request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $ 9,295 Plaintiff Bank of America has failed to show that Lipson was validly served pursuant to CCP § 415.20(b), because the declaration of diligence accompanying the proof of service states that Lipton was employed at that address and personal service was attempted there, while Lipson's declaration that he neither resided nor worked at that address is uncontradicted (Lipson Decl. 3) and the server's notation was admittedly erroneous (Mughal Decl.). Therefore pursuant to CCP § 473(d) the default judgment is vacated as void. (Dill v. Berquist Construction Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1426, 1439.) Because plaintiff's counsel was aware that the declaration of diligence accompanying the proof of service did not meet the Local Rule 6.6(c) requirements for due diligence declaration by a server in support for a request for default but nevertheless proceeded to obtain entry of default and default judgment thus causing unnecessary motion practice by Lipson, Lipson is awarded $ 9,295 as reasonable attorney fees incurred for filing the motions to quash service and to vacate default judgment per CCP § 575.2. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lipson is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. MICHAEL LIPSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Process And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT MICHAEL LIPSON'S Motion To Quash Service Of Process And Request For Sanctions. Defendant Michael Lipson's motion to quash service of process is GRANTED. Plaintiff Bank of America has failed to show that Lipson was validly served pursuant to CCP § 415.20(b), because the declaration of diligence accompanying the proof of service states that Lipson was employed at that address and personal service was attempted there, while Lipson's declaration that he neither resided nor worked at that address is uncontradicted (Lipson Decl. 3) and the server's notation was admittedly erroneous (Mughal Decl.). Lipson shall provide to plaintiff an address where he can be properly served. Lipson's request for sanctions is procedurally improper under CCP §§ 128.7 (c)(1) and 128.5 (f)(1), and his request for sanctions under CCP § 575.2 is addressed in the court's ruling on Lipson's motion to vacate default judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lipson is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: KANSAI DEVELOPMENT, INC, ET AL VS. DAVID S. LEE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Lee Law Firm'S Motion For Order To Compel Binding Arbitration As To All Parties And Stay Action.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 11, 2019, Line 9, Defendant The Lee Law Firm'S Motion For Order To Compel Binding Arbitration As To All Parties And Stay Action. Defendants' (hereafter "Lee") motion to compel arbitration and stay is GRANTED. The evidence establishes that the entities that are seeking arbitration are the same as the entity that entered into the arbitration agreement with plaintiffs. (Lee Reply Dec., par. 5.) The motion to compel arbitration is timely and Rosenson v. Greenberg Glusker Fields Claman & Machtinger LLP (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 688, 691 is inapposite. There, the court explains that the 30-day deadline applies to one seeking to avoid the Mandatory Fee Arbitration Act award. In this case, the award was in Lee's favor and Lee was not rejecting it. After plaintiffs rejected the award, "the action shall proceed in accordance with the provisions of Par 2 (commencing with section 307) of the Code of Civil Procedure, concerning civil actions generally." (Business & Professions Code § 6204(c).) The supplemental declaration of Sergio Stevens establishes that the Lee Law Firm is registered with the State Bar. (Stevens Dec., Ex. 3 and 4.) In addition, the failure of a firm to register with the State Bar in compliance with Business & Professions Code § 6160 is not a ground to avoid arbitration. Such dereliction is ordinarily not the type of "inequitable" conduct or unclean hands/bad faith that compels denial a motion to compel arbitration. In Weisman v. Johnson (1982) 133 Cal.App.3d 289, the court denied arbitration because the party seeking arbitration engaged in delay tactics and made misstatements about his residence. The failure to properly register as a law corporation with the Bar has no effect on the recovery of fees. (See Olson v. Cohen (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 1209.) "Incorporation as a professional corporation is not undertaken for the protection of clients." (Steven M. Garber & Associates v. Eskandarian (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 813, 820.) Failing to register is a situation drastically different from practicing law in California without a license. There is no evidence that the members of the Lee Law Firm practiced law in California without a proper license. The court further notes that California has a "strong public policy in favor of arbitration." (Ahdout v. Hekmatjah (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 21, 33.) Plaintiffs' argument that defendants violated CCP 1281.2 by failing to demand arbitration fails. That section provides that "[o]n petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party thereto refuses to arbitrate such controversy¿" In this case, defendants are proceeding by motion, not "petition." Plaintiffs' filing of this action showed its refusal to arbitrate. Lastly, plaintiffs fail to show that the arbitration or retainer agreement is "illegal" such that it cannot be enforced. As noted, defendants have California bar licenses, were registered with the State Bar, and were not "out of state" attorneys illegally practicing law in California. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
